nativeRange.habitat: Lagoon and seaward reef environments; tends to hide in crevices under rocks and coral formations during the day and hunt at night

diet.notes: Primarily feeds on live shrimp, small fish, and crustaceans. When first introduced, live saltwater feeder shrimp are generally advised to encourage feeding. With patience, many individuals have been known to transition to frozen meaty foods such as silversides, krill, and shrimp. Ornamental shrimp in the aquarium are typically at risk of predation.

avoidWith: small fish that fit in mouth
other lionfish unless adequate space provided

imageCandidates.notes: Christian Mehlfuehrer; 2400x1600. Tack-sharp full-body view against a dark background; fan-like pectorals fully spread, banded feathery rays and dark spots on the fin membranes clearly visible, vibrant red-and-white barring. Most striking shot.
Nick Hobgood; 1920x1441. Sharp natural in-situ shot on sand; textbook diagnostic dark spots on pectoral-fin membranes, long supraocular tentacles, red-white vertical bars.
NasserHalaweh; 2799x2099. Sharp, vibrant aquarium view from a different angle; whole fish in frame with separated feathery pectoral rays and membrane spots visible.

summary: The Antennata Lionfish (Pterois antennata) is an Indo-Pacific predator known for its fan-like pectoral fins and venomous dorsal spines. Generally considered intermediate in care, it is nocturnal by nature and benefits from ample hiding places in a reef or FOWLR aquarium.
careNotes:
The Antennata Lionfish is a nocturnal ambush predator found across the Indo-Pacific, from East Africa to the Marquesas Islands, at depths of roughly 2–86 m in lagoon and seaward reef habitats. It tends to rest during the day in rock crevices or under ledges and becomes active at night to hunt shrimps and crabs.

Venomous spines: The dorsal, pelvic, and anal fin spines are venomous and capable of inflicting a painful sting. Stings have been described as more painful than a bee sting; treating the affected area with water as hot as is safely tolerable and seeking medical attention is advisable.

Feeding transition: Many individuals initially accept only live foods. Patience and a gradual introduction of frozen meaty foods — silversides, krill, frozen shrimp, and crustacean flesh — is generally recommended. Target feeding with a feeding stick tends to work well for weaning individuals onto prepared foods.

Reef compatibility: This species will generally consume ornamental shrimp and small fish. It tends to leave corals and sessile invertebrates (animals such as anemones and tube worms that remain fixed in place) undisturbed, but placement in a mixed-reef system with vulnerable invertebrates warrants careful consideration.
breedingNotes: In the wild, the Antennata Lionfish reproduces through pelagic broadcast spawning — a method in which males and females engage in courtship displays before releasing eggs and sperm freely into the water column, where fertilization occurs. Captive breeding is uncommon and has rarely been reported outside of public aquarium settings.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing (20 cm TL max), native range (East Africa to Marquesas), temperature (24.6–29°C preferred), taxonomy, and habitat. FishBase's temperature range is wider than the hobby-source consensus.

additional.name: Reeflings
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (20 cm), temperature (24–26°C), salinity (1.023–1.026), tank minimum 200 L, care level moderate, semi-aggressive temperament. Reef safe listed as 'No' vs 'With Caution' on other sources; majority 'with caution' was used.

additional.name: AquaticCommunity
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (20 cm / 7.9 in), temperature (72–78°F / 22–25.5°C), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (1.020–1.025). Minimum tank listed as 30 gallons / 115 L — notably smaller than LiveAquaria (120 gal). Values averaged per playbook rules.

fish.breedingNotes: Reproduces through pelagic broadcast spawning in the wild, with courtship displays observed before egg and sperm release. Captive breeding is rarely documented.
